title: Hot and Sour Soup

seo.title: Authentic Hot and Sour Soup Recipe | Easy Chinese Soup
seo.metaDescription: Craving a delicious bowl of Hot and Sour Soup? Learn how to make this authentic Chinese classic at home with our easy-to-follow recipe. Perfect for a cozy meal!

easy Hot and Sour Soup recipe
authentic Chinese Hot and Sour Soup recipe
Hot and Sour Soup recipe step by step
best homemade Hot and Sour Soup
seo.h1: Authentic Hot and Sour Soup Recipe: A Chinese Classic
h2Headings: What is Hot and Sour Soup?
Pro Tips for the Best Hot and Sour Soup
seo.imageAlt: A steaming bowl of authentic Chinese Hot and Sour Soup, garnished with green onions and chili oil.
seo.imageCaption: Enjoy a comforting bowl of homemade Hot and Sour Soup, bursting with savory, spicy, and tangy flavors.

description: Experience the exhilarating balance of heat and tang in this classic Chinese Hot and Sour Soup. This comforting bowl features tender tofu, earthy mushrooms, and a rich broth, all perfectly seasoned to awaken your taste buds. A truly satisfying and authentic culinary journey awaits with every spoonful.

ingredients.notes: mixed with 3 tbsp cold water to form a slurry

instructions.title: Prepare Ingredients
instructions.text: Rehydrate dried shiitake and wood ear mushrooms in hot water for 20-30 minutes, then drain, stem shiitakes, and slice both. Slice pork or chicken and tofu into thin matchsticks. Prepare the cornstarch slurry and beat the eggs. Have all other seasonings ready.

instructions.title: Simmer the Broth
instructions.text: In a large pot, bring the chicken or vegetable broth to a gentle simmer over medium heat. Add the sliced pork or chicken, mushrooms, bamboo shoots, and tofu. Cook for 5-7 minutes, until the meat is cooked through.

instructions.title: Season the Soup
instructions.text: Stir in the soy sauce, rice vinegar, and white pepper. Taste and adjust seasonings as needed, adding more vinegar for tang or white pepper for heat. If desired, add a pinch of salt.

instructions.title: Thicken the Soup
instructions.text: Slowly whisk in the cornstarch slurry, stirring continuously until the soup thickens to your desired consistency. This usually takes 1-2 minutes. Ensure no lumps form.

instructions.title: Add Eggs and Finish
instructions.text: While the soup is simmering, slowly pour the beaten eggs into the soup in a thin stream, stirring gently in one direction with chopsticks or a fork to create delicate egg ribbons. Remove from heat immediately.

instructions.title: Serve and Garnish
instructions.text: Stir in the sesame oil and optional chili oil. Ladle the Hot and Sour Soup into individual bowls. Garnish generously with chopped green onions before serving hot.

tips: For authentic flavor, do not skimp on the white pepper and rice vinegar; they are the heart of hot and sour soup. Adjust them gradually to find your perfect balance.
Using a good quality chicken or pork broth is crucial for the depth of flavor. If using vegetable broth, consider adding a dash of mushroom powder for umami.
When adding the egg, pour slowly and stir gently in one direction. This creates long, delicate strands of egg flower rather than chunky pieces.
article:
Embrace the tantalizing dance of flavors that is Hot and Sour Soup, a culinary masterpiece that has captivated palates worldwide. This iconic Chinese dish is more than just a soup; it's an experience, a symphony of spicy, tangy, savory, and umami notes that awaken the senses. From bustling street markets in Beijing to elegant dining rooms across the globe, Hot and Sour Soup stands as a testament to the intricate balance and depth found within Chinese cuisine.

Its distinct character makes it a beloved appetizer or a comforting meal on its own, offering warmth and invigoration with every spoonful. Whether you’re a seasoned chef or a home cook looking to explore the rich tapestry of Asian flavors, mastering this soup is a rewarding journey. This guide will walk you through everything you need to know to create an authentic and unforgettable bowl of Hot and Sour Soup right in your own kitchen.

## What is Hot and Sour Soup?

At its heart, Hot and Sour Soup is a traditional Chinese soup celebrated for its complex flavor profile and intriguing texture. The "hot" typically comes from white pepper and, optionally, chili oil or flakes, providing a pungent warmth rather than an overwhelming burn. The "sour" is derived from rice vinegar, which lends a bright, acidic counterpoint that cuts through the richness of the broth. Beyond these two dominant flavors, the soup is rich in umami, thanks to ingredients like soy sauce, mushrooms, and often a savory broth base.

Visually, it's a feast for the eyes, with its characteristic dark hue and a medley of finely sliced ingredients suspended in a thickened broth. Common components include slivers of pork or chicken, delicate strands of egg flower, bamboo shoots for a crisp bite, and various mushrooms such as wood ear and shiitake, which contribute to both flavor and texture. The broth is usually thickened with cornstarch, giving it a silky, luxurious mouthfeel that coats the palate beautifully. While its exact origins are debated, with some attributing it to Sichuan province and others to Beijing, its global popularity is undeniable, making it a staple on Chinese restaurant menus everywhere.

## Ingredients You'll Need

Crafting an authentic Hot and Sour Soup requires a thoughtful selection of ingredients, each playing a crucial role in achieving that signature flavor and texture. Don't be intimidated by the list; many of these are readily available in most grocery stores or Asian markets.

*   **For the Broth:**
    *   6 cups Chicken or Vegetable Broth (low sodium preferred)
*   **Proteins & Vegetables:**
    *   4 oz Pork Loin or Chicken Breast, thinly sliced into matchsticks
    *   8 oz Firm Tofu, pressed and cut into small cubes or matchsticks
    *   4-5 dried Shiitake Mushrooms, rehydrated and thinly sliced (reserve soaking liquid)
    *   1/2 cup dried Wood Ear Mushrooms, rehydrated and thinly sliced (or fresh if available)
    *   1/2 cup Bamboo Shoots, thinly sliced
    *   2 large eggs, beaten
*   **Seasonings & Flavor Base:**
    *   1/4 cup Soy Sauce (light or regular)
    *   1/4 cup Rice Vinegar (or more to taste)
    *   2-3 tsp White Pepper (crucial for the "hot" element)
    *   1-2 tsp Sesame Oil
    *   1-2 tsp Chili Oil (optional, for extra heat)
    *   1 tbsp Fresh Ginger, grated (optional, for added depth)
*   **Thickener:**
    *   3 tbsp Cornstarch, dissolved in 1/4 cup cold water (cornstarch slurry)
*   **Garnish:**
    *   2 Green Onions, thinly sliced

## Step-by-Step Instructions

Making a delicious Hot and Sour Soup from scratch is a surprisingly straightforward process. Follow these steps for a perfect bowl:

1.  **Prepare Ingredients:** If using dried mushrooms, rehydrate them according to package directions. Slice all proteins and vegetables into uniform, thin matchsticks or cubes. Beat the eggs lightly in a small bowl. Prepare your cornstarch slurry by whisking cornstarch with cold water until smooth.
2.  **Start the Broth:** Pour the chicken or vegetable broth into a large pot or Dutch oven. Add the reserved mushroom soaking liquid (if using dried shiitake) for extra umami. Bring the broth to a gentle boil over medium-high heat.
3.  **Add Proteins & Vegetables:** Once the broth is boiling, add the sliced pork (or chicken) and cook for 1-2 minutes until no longer pink. Then, add the tofu, bamboo shoots, and all sliced mushrooms. Reduce heat to a simmer and cook for 5 minutes to allow flavors to meld.
4.  **Season the Soup:** Stir in the soy sauce, rice vinegar, and white pepper. Taste and adjust the "hot" and "sour" elements to your preference, adding more white pepper or vinegar if desired. This is where the characteristic Hot and Sour Soup flavor truly comes alive.
5.  **Thicken the Broth:** Slowly drizzle the cornstarch slurry into the simmering soup while continuously stirring. Continue stirring until the soup thickens to your desired consistency. You might not need all of the slurry, or you might need a little more if you prefer a thicker soup.
6.  **Swirl in Egg:** With the soup still simmering gently, slowly pour the beaten eggs into the soup in a thin stream while stirring the soup in a circular motion. This creates beautiful, delicate egg "flowers" or ribbons throughout the soup.
7.  **Finish & Serve:** Remove the soup from the heat. Stir in the sesame oil and chili oil (if using). Ladle the Hot and Sour Soup into bowls and garnish generously with sliced green onions. Serve immediately.

## Pro Tips for the Best Hot and Sour Soup

Achieving that perfect balance in your Hot and Sour Soup can be elevated with a few insider tips:

*   **Don't Skimp on White Pepper:** White pepper is the primary source of "heat" in traditional Hot and Sour Soup. Its unique pungency is distinct from chili heat. Don't be afraid to add a generous amount; it's what gives the soup its authentic kick.
*   **Taste and Adjust:** The beauty of this soup lies in its customizable balance. Continuously taste the soup after adding the vinegar and white pepper. Some prefer it tangier, others spicier. Adjust these elements to suit your personal preference.
*   **Quality Broth Matters:** A good quality chicken or vegetable broth forms the foundation of your soup. If using store-bought, opt for low-sodium to control the saltiness.
*   **Cornstarch Slurry Technique:** Always dissolve cornstarch in cold water before adding it to hot liquid to prevent lumps. Add it slowly and stir constantly until the desired thickness is reached. If the soup cools down and thickens too much, you can thin it with a little more broth.
*   **Egg Flower Method:** For delicate egg ribbons, pour the beaten egg in a very thin stream into the simmering soup while gently stirring in one direction. Avoid vigorous stirring, which can break up the egg too much.
*   **Fresh Ingredients:** While dried mushrooms are traditional and add depth, using fresh mushrooms (like shiitake and wood ear) can enhance the texture and freshness of the soup.

## Serving Suggestions

Hot and Sour Soup is incredibly versatile and can be enjoyed in various ways:

*   **As an Appetizer:** It's a classic starter for any Chinese meal, perfectly whetting the appetite for subsequent dishes.
*   **Light Meal:** A generous bowl of Hot and Sour Soup, especially with extra tofu and vegetables, can be a satisfying and warming light meal on its own.
*   **Pair with Rice or Noodles:** Serve it alongside a bowl of steamed white rice or plain noodles for a more substantial meal. The soup's rich flavor complements the simplicity of the starch.
*   **Complete Chinese Feast:** Incorporate it into a larger spread of Chinese dishes, such as fried rice, spring rolls, or stir-fries, to create a balanced and diverse dining experience.

## Variations and Substitutions

One of the great joys of cooking Hot and Sour Soup is its adaptability. Feel free to experiment and make it your own:

*   **Vegetarian/Vegan:** Easily make this soup meat-free by using vegetable broth and omitting the pork. Increase the amount of tofu and add extra mushrooms (like king oyster or enoki) for more texture and umami. Ensure your soy sauce is vegan-friendly.
*   **Different Proteins:** Instead of pork, you can use thinly sliced chicken breast, shredded duck, or even shrimp for a seafood twist. Add shrimp in the last few minutes of cooking to prevent overcooking.
*   **Add More Vegetables:** Feel free to incorporate other vegetables like thinly sliced carrots, water chestnuts, snow peas, or even finely chopped cabbage for added nutrition and texture.
*   **Spice Level:** For those who crave more heat, a dash of chili garlic sauce, dried red chili flakes, or a few drops of tabasco can be stirred in at the end.
*   **Gluten-Free:** Ensure all your sauces (soy sauce, broth) are certified gluten-free. Tamari is an excellent gluten-free substitute for soy sauce.
*   **Lily Flowers:** Traditionally, dried lily flowers (also known as golden needles) are used. If you can find them in an Asian market, rehydrate and add them for an authentic floral note and unique texture.

Hot and Sour Soup is more than just a recipe; it's an exploration of Chinese culinary philosophy – a harmonious blend of contrasting elements that come together to create something truly magical. By following these steps and tips, you're not just cooking a soup; you're crafting an experience that will delight your senses and transport you to the heart of Chinese gastronomy. So gather your ingredients, fire up your stove, and prepare to savor the exquisite complexity of homemade Hot and Sour Soup.
